{{translated page|ja|杉山神社|version=106747028|comment=Imported full ja history}}[[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] == Japanese content == '''Sugiyama Shrine''' (杉山神社, *Sugiyama-jinja*) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} that primarily enshrines {{jalink|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|Q10881556}} (a son of {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993}}) and {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} as its {{jalink|principal deities|主祭神}}.<ref name="akanedai-sugiyama"> {{Wayback |url=http://www.akanedai.net/sugiyama.php |title=杉山神社について |date=20140801182804}}akanedai.net. Accessed January 12, 2020</ref><ref>[http://rekisisuki.exblog.jp/15473809/ 杉山神社 大棚社 港北ニュータウン] (History and Pleasant Associations)</ref> It is considered one of the shrines listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Engishiki shrine register}} of the former {{jalink|Musashi Province|Q907398}}; however, there are several dozen shrines regarded as its {{jalink|candidate shrines|Q135022904}} (ronsha), mainly concentrated in present-day {{jalink|Yokohama|Q38283}} and elsewhere in {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}, as well as {{jalink|Kawasaki|Q164234}}, {{jalink|Machida|Q210628}}, and {{jalink|Inagi|Q317847}} in {{jalink|Tokyo Metropolis|Q1490}}.<ref name="akanedai-sugiyamalist"> {{Wayback |url=http://www.akanedai.net/sugiyama_list.php |title=杉山神社リスト |date=20170706050732}}akanedai.net. Accessed January 12, 2020</ref> The shrine name is sometimes written as '''椙山神社''' as well.
=== Overview === Sugiyama Shrine is said to have expanded primarily along the {{jalink|Tsurumi River|Q7850292}} {{jalink|river system|Q285451}} as a center of popular worship for people living in the surrounding region, but it remains a shrine with many unresolved mysteries. In addition to the Tsurumi River, shrines are found along the {{jalink|Katabira River|Q11481286}}, the {{jalink|Ōoka River (Kanagawa)|Q8081676}}, and on the right bank of the {{jalink|Tama River|Q1063006}} (in Kawasaki and Inagi), but none exist beyond the Tama River.
According to the *{{jalink|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|Q11503943}}*, compiled in the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, there were once 72 Sugiyama Shrines in total. Due to later {{jalink|shrine mergers|Q11412524}} and name changes, however, the number of shrines currently registered as {{jalink|religious corporations|Q7311343}} bearing the name “Sugiyama” (including the variant “Sugiyama/椙山”) stands at 44.<ref name="akanedai-sugiyama"/><ref name="akanedai-sugiyamalist"/><ref name="hodogayakyoudoshi-sugiyama">[http://www.shinmeisya.or.jp/rekisi/sugiyama_01.html 杉山神社考] (Shinmei Shrine: Hodogaya Local History)</ref>
In the *{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}*, compiled in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japanese era)|Q1075635}} 11 ({{jalink|869|Q23917}}) of the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, the shrine appears as “'''Fusuyama Shrine''' (枌山神社),” indicating its antiquity. The same text records that the shrine received {{jalink|imperial offerings|官幣}} in {{jalink|Jōwa (Japanese era)|Q1075605}} 5 ({{jalink|838|Q23838}}), second month, and was granted the rank of {{jalink|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|従五位下}} in Jōwa 15 ({{jalink|848|Q30380}}), fifth month.<ref name="hodogayakyoudoshi-sugiyama"/> Furthermore, the *{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}*, compiled in {{jalink|Engi (era)|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), describes it as “'''the sole Engishiki-listed shrine in Tsuzuki District of Musashi Province'''.” However, the location of the original main shrine has never been definitively identified, and many candidate shrines still exist. Among them, four shrines located in Yokohama—specifically in {{jalink|Midori Ward, Yokohama|Q1203373}} ({{jalink|Nishihassaku|Q86744095}}), {{jalink|Tsuzuki Ward|Q1153920}} (Nakagawa and {{jalink|Chigasaki Chūō|Q11617146}}), and {{jalink|Kōhoku Ward|Q1358965}} (Shin-Yoshida)—are currently regarded as the most plausible candidates.<ref name="yashiro-sugiyama">[http://skyimpulse.s26.xrea.com/sugiyama.html 杉山神社巡り] (Reflections on the Shrines of the Gods)</ref> {{See also|Tsurumi Shrine (Yokohama)#Sugiyama Shrine and Tsurumi Shrine}}
As for the origin of the name, several theories exist: that the shrine was enshrined on a cedar-covered mountain; that it derives from Isotakeru-no-Mikoto, a deity of trees, and cedar groves; or that it comes from cedar trees used as {{jalink|shipbuilding|船舶}} material.<ref name="tsugarukodai-sugiyama"> {{Wayback |url=http://www18.ocn.ne.jp/~tugaru/sikinaisyasugiyama.html |title=武蔵国都筑郡唯一の式内社杉山神社を推理 |date=20140810041733}} Accessed January 12, 2020 (Tsugaru Ancient Kingdom)</ref>
Although many aspects of the shrine’s origins remain unclear, one theory holds that descendants of the {{jalink|Izumo Province|Q582846}} people (Isotakeru-no-Mikoto) led a maritime group from {{jalink|Kii Province|Q864053}} {{jalink|Kumano|Q6443450}}, arrived at the {{jalink|Izu Peninsula|Q822822}} and {{jalink|Miura Peninsula|Q536757}}, and, via the latter, settled along the Tsurumi River system, where their leader founded Sugiyama Shrine.<ref name="tsugarukodai-sugiyama"/>
In {{jalink|Hayama, Miura District|Q1342807}} of the former {{jalink|Sagami Province|Q1053732}}, there exists the only Sugiyama Shrine in that province, along with a so-called “original shrine” (*motomiya*) enshrined in the surrounding mountains.<ref name="tsugarukodai-sugiyama"/><ref>{{Wayback |url=http://www.akanedai.net/sugiyama_65.php |title=杉山神社 (三浦郡葉山町) |date=20140808213836}}akanedai.net. Accessed January 12, 2020</ref> However, its relationship to the Sugiyama Shrines of Musashi Province remains unclear.
Another tradition, preserved at the {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Chigasaki Chūō, Tsuzuki Ward, Yokohama)|Q17994298}}, states that in the third year of the {{jalink|Hakuhō|Q11580612}} era under {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}}, three deities—{{jalink|Takami-Musubi|高御産日命}}, {{jalink|Ame-no-Hiwashi|Q10939697}}, and {{jalink|Abawakehiko-no-Mikoto|由布津主命}}—were enshrined on Sugiyama Hill in Musashi Province by Imibe no Katsumaro, a priest of {{jalink|Awa Shrine|Q705547}} of the {{jalink|Imbe clan|Q8189229}} from Kii Province. As the Imbe expanded their cultivation of hemp and grain, the shrine is said to have spread as well.
At that time, Tsuzuki District was strongly influenced by the Imbe clan, pioneer settlers who formed a small polity outside the control of the Musashi provincial government, constructing numerous {{jalink|kofun|Q1141225}} burial mounds and moated settlements. It also served as a distribution hub crossed by the ancient Tōkaidō route. However, although the *Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō* records that the shrine was founded by Imibe no Katsumaro, there are no surviving {{jalink|historical documentary sources|Q3771738}} to substantiate this claim, and it is considered weakly supported.<ref name="akanedai-sugiyama"/><ref name="yashiro-sugiyama" />
Separately from the above origin theories, there is also a tradition linking Sugiyama Shrine to {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}}. In Sugiyama of {{jalink|Doi District, Sagami Province|Q11423859}}, seven small shrines dedicated to {{jalink|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|Q11059454}} ({{jalink|Benzaiten}}) were established to protect the {{jalink|demon gate|Q11673539}} of {{jalink|Kamakura|Q10939227}}; these were collectively known as the “Seven Sugiyama Shrines.” Some of these overlap with candidate Engishiki Sugiyama Shrines.<ref>[http://hamarepo.com/story.php?story_id=1602? Yokohama City’s Scattered Sugiyama Shrines—A Complete Tour! vol.2] (Hamarepo.com, January 19, 2013)</ref>
Additionally, {{jalink|Ejima Sugiyama Shrine|Q97280595}} in {{jalink|Chitose, Sumida Ward|Q11405622}} of {{jalink|Tokyo|Q1490}} was originally a {{jalink|Benten shrine|弁天社}} (established by transferring a deity from {{jalink|Enoshima Shrine}}). The name “Sugiyama” derives from the enshrinement of {{jalink|Sugiyama Waichi|Q7635129}}.
=== Major Sugiyama Shrines in the Kantō Region === ; Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture * {{jalink|Tsurumi Shrine (Yokohama)|Q11676163}} ({{jalink|Tsurumi Ward|Q1202820}}, {{jalink|Tsurumi-chūō|Q30924832}}) – former name * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Kishiya|Q11476016}}, Tsurumi Ward) * {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Katakura, Kanagawa Ward, Yokohama)|Q24863397}} * Sugiyama Ōkami ({{jalink|Rokkakubashi|Q11392542}}, Kanagawa Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Sugata-chō|Q96482916}}, Kanagawa Ward) * {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Nishi Ward, Yokohama)|Q11521946}} – also known as Tobe Sugiyama Shrine * {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Minami-Ōta, Yokohama)|Q134987090}} – also known as Yokohama Suitengū * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Miyamoto-chō|Q11453265}}, Minami Ward) * {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Kawashima-chō, Hodogaya Ward)|Q11521944}} – also known as Kawashima Sugiyama Shrine * {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Hoshikawa, Hodogaya Ward)|Q17994302}} – also known as Hoshikawa Sugiyama Shrine * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Wada|Q17216949}}, Hodogaya Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Hotoke-mukai-chō|Q11377506}}, Hodogaya Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Kamihoshikawa|Q11359094}}, Hodogaya Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Nishikubo-chō|Q85883633}}, Hodogaya Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Sakamoto-chō|Q97188018}}, Hodogaya Ward) * '''{{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Shin-Yoshida, Kōhoku Ward)|Q17994294}}''' – one of the leading candidate shrines * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Kishine-chō|Q11475945}}, Kōhoku Ward) * {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Niihama-chō, Kōhoku Ward)|}} – general guardian shrine of Niihama * Sugiyama Shrine (Tarumachi 4-chōme, Kōhoku Ward) * '''{{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Nishihassaku, Midori Ward)|Q11521945}}''' – one of the leading candidate shrines; participates in the {{jalink|Kurayami Festival|Q11265601}} of {{jalink|Ōkunitama Shrine|Q611678}} as the sixth-ranked shrine of Musashi Province * Sugiyama Shrine (Nakayama-chō, Midori Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Aoto-chō|Q28686651}}, Midori Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Kamoi|Q11675526}}, Midori Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Miho-chō|Q86744085}}, Midori Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine (Terayama-chō, Midori Ward) * {{jalink|Chigusadai Sugiyama Shrine|Q22119381}} ({{jalink|Aoba Ward|Q1203361}}) * {{jalink|Ichigao Sugiyama Shrine|Q22119100}} (Ichigao-chō, Aoba Ward) * {{jalink|Mitakedai Sugiyama Shrine}} (Mitakedai, Aoba Ward) * Kamionda Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Akane-dai|Q11256888}}, Aoba Ward) * Tetsu Shrine ({{jalink|Tetsu-chō|Q11649584}}, Aoba Ward) – formerly Aoki Myōjin and Sugiyama Myōjin combined * '''{{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Chigasaki, Tsuzuki Ward)|Q17994298}}''' – leading candidate shrine * '''{{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Nakagawa, Tsuzuki Ward)|Q17994300}}''' – leading candidate shrine; also known as Ōdana Sugiyama Shrine * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Katsuta-chō|Q97187870}}, Tsuzuki Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Ōkuma-chō|Q97188121}}, Tsuzuki Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Saedo-chō|Q13641379}}, Tsuzuki Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Ikebe-chō|Q11552287}}, Tsuzuki Ward)
; Kanagawa Prefecture (outside Yokohama) * Sugiyama Ōkami ({{jalink|Ogura|Q11458882}}, {{jalink|Saiwai Ward|Q1153579}}, Kawasaki) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Suenaga|Q11519801}}, {{jalink|Takatsu Ward|Q1076736}}, Kawasaki) – shrine buildings destroyed by fire in {{jalink|1964|Q2652}} and rebuilt in {{jalink|1972|Q2476}} * Sugiyama Shrine (Nishi-Ikuta, {{jalink|Tama Ward|Q1076769}}, Kawasaki) * Sugiyama Shrine (Kamiyamaguchi, {{jalink|Hayama|Q1342807}}, Miura District)
; Tokyo Metropolis * {{jalink|Ejima Sugiyama Shrine|Q97280595}} ({{jalink|Chitose|Q11405622}}, {{jalink|Sumida Ward|Q235135}}) – enshrines Sugiyama Kengyō ({{jalink|Sugiyama Waichi|Q7635129}}) * Sugiyama Shrine (Hirao, Inagi) * {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Miwa-chō, Machida)|}} * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Naruse|Q11495526}}, Machida) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Kanamori|Q11647251}}, Machida) – two shrines in the same district * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Tsukushino|Q11272186}}, Machida) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Nogaya|Q11611864}}, Machida) – Tsukushino branch office
=== Sugiyama Shrines outside the Kantō Region === * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Hyakuzawa|Q11580777}}, {{jalink|Hirosaki|Q194087}}, {{jalink|Aomori Prefecture|Q71699}}) * {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Sakurai City)|}} (Iimorizuka, {{jalink|Sakurai|Q127026}}, {{jalink|Nara Prefecture|Q131287}}) – enshrines {{jalink|Ōyamatsumi|大山祇神}}, with {{jalink|Ame-no-Minakanushi}} and {{jalink|Kamimusubi}} also enshrined * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Nishijin|Q11628294}}, {{jalink|Sawara Ward|Q1071779}}, {{jalink|Fukuoka City|Q26600}}, {{jalink|Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258}}) – an {{jalink|Inari shrine|Q514480}} enshrining {{jalink|Ukanomitama}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = Sukiyamano Shrine | native_name = 杉山神社 | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Sukiyamano Shrine | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }}
{{nihongo|'''Sukiyamano Shrine'''|杉山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Musashi Province]].
== instance of (P31) ==

== topic's main category (P910) ==

==References==
[[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigesha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Sukiyamano Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]]
[[JA:杉山神社]]
{{wikidata link|Q135039726}} [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]]
{{translated page|ja|杉山神社|version=106747028|comment=Imported full ja history}}== Japanese content == '''Sugiyama Shrine''' (杉山神社, *Sugiyama-jinja*) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} that primarily enshrines {{jalink|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|Q10881556}} (a son of {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993}}) and {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} as its {{jalink|principal deities|主祭神}}.<ref name="akanedai-sugiyama"> {{Wayback |url=http://www.akanedai.net/sugiyama.php |title=杉山神社について |date=20140801182804}}akanedai.net. Accessed January 12, 2020</ref><ref>[http://rekisisuki.exblog.jp/15473809/ 杉山神社 大棚社 港北ニュータウン] (History and Pleasant Associations)</ref> It is considered one of the shrines listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Engishiki shrine register}} of the former {{jalink|Musashi Province|Q907398}}; however, there are several dozen shrines regarded as its {{jalink|candidate shrines|Q135022904}} (ronsha), mainly concentrated in present-day {{jalink|Yokohama|Q38283}} and elsewhere in {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}, as well as {{jalink|Kawasaki|Q164234}}, {{jalink|Machida|Q210628}}, and {{jalink|Inagi|Q317847}} in {{jalink|Tokyo Metropolis|Q1490}}.<ref name="akanedai-sugiyamalist"> {{Wayback |url=http://www.akanedai.net/sugiyama_list.php |title=杉山神社リスト |date=20170706050732}}akanedai.net. Accessed January 12, 2020</ref> The shrine name is sometimes written as '''椙山神社''' as well.
=== Overview === Sugiyama Shrine is said to have expanded primarily along the {{jalink|Tsurumi River|Q7850292}} {{jalink|river system|Q285451}} as a center of popular worship for people living in the surrounding region, but it remains a shrine with many unresolved mysteries. In addition to the Tsurumi River, shrines are found along the {{jalink|Katabira River|Q11481286}}, the {{jalink|Ōoka River (Kanagawa)|Q8081676}}, and on the right bank of the {{jalink|Tama River|Q1063006}} (in Kawasaki and Inagi), but none exist beyond the Tama River.
According to the *{{jalink|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|Q11503943}}*, compiled in the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, there were once 72 Sugiyama Shrines in total. Due to later {{jalink|shrine mergers|Q11412524}} and name changes, however, the number of shrines currently registered as {{jalink|religious corporations|Q7311343}} bearing the name “Sugiyama” (including the variant “Sugiyama/椙山”) stands at 44.<ref name="akanedai-sugiyama"/><ref name="akanedai-sugiyamalist"/><ref name="hodogayakyoudoshi-sugiyama">[http://www.shinmeisya.or.jp/rekisi/sugiyama_01.html 杉山神社考] (Shinmei Shrine: Hodogaya Local History)</ref>
In the *{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}*, compiled in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japanese era)|Q1075635}} 11 ({{jalink|869|Q23917}}) of the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, the shrine appears as “'''Fusuyama Shrine''' (枌山神社),” indicating its antiquity. The same text records that the shrine received {{jalink|imperial offerings|官幣}} in {{jalink|Jōwa (Japanese era)|Q1075605}} 5 ({{jalink|838|Q23838}}), second month, and was granted the rank of {{jalink|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|従五位下}} in Jōwa 15 ({{jalink|848|Q30380}}), fifth month.<ref name="hodogayakyoudoshi-sugiyama"/> Furthermore, the *{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}*, compiled in {{jalink|Engi (era)|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), describes it as “'''the sole Engishiki-listed shrine in Tsuzuki District of Musashi Province'''.” However, the location of the original main shrine has never been definitively identified, and many candidate shrines still exist. Among them, four shrines located in Yokohama—specifically in {{jalink|Midori Ward, Yokohama|Q1203373}} ({{jalink|Nishihassaku|Q86744095}}), {{jalink|Tsuzuki Ward|Q1153920}} (Nakagawa and {{jalink|Chigasaki Chūō|Q11617146}}), and {{jalink|Kōhoku Ward|Q1358965}} (Shin-Yoshida)—are currently regarded as the most plausible candidates.<ref name="yashiro-sugiyama">[http://skyimpulse.s26.xrea.com/sugiyama.html 杉山神社巡り] (Reflections on the Shrines of the Gods)</ref> {{See also|Tsurumi Shrine (Yokohama)#Sugiyama Shrine and Tsurumi Shrine}}
As for the origin of the name, several theories exist: that the shrine was enshrined on a cedar-covered mountain; that it derives from Isotakeru-no-Mikoto, a deity of trees, and cedar groves; or that it comes from cedar trees used as {{jalink|shipbuilding|船舶}} material.<ref name="tsugarukodai-sugiyama"> {{Wayback |url=http://www18.ocn.ne.jp/~tugaru/sikinaisyasugiyama.html |title=武蔵国都筑郡唯一の式内社杉山神社を推理 |date=20140810041733}} Accessed January 12, 2020 (Tsugaru Ancient Kingdom)</ref>
Although many aspects of the shrine’s origins remain unclear, one theory holds that descendants of the {{jalink|Izumo Province|Q582846}} people (Isotakeru-no-Mikoto) led a maritime group from {{jalink|Kii Province|Q864053}} {{jalink|Kumano|Q6443450}}, arrived at the {{jalink|Izu Peninsula|Q822822}} and {{jalink|Miura Peninsula|Q536757}}, and, via the latter, settled along the Tsurumi River system, where their leader founded Sugiyama Shrine.<ref name="tsugarukodai-sugiyama"/>
In {{jalink|Hayama, Miura District|Q1342807}} of the former {{jalink|Sagami Province|Q1053732}}, there exists the only Sugiyama Shrine in that province, along with a so-called “original shrine” (*motomiya*) enshrined in the surrounding mountains.<ref name="tsugarukodai-sugiyama"/><ref>{{Wayback |url=http://www.akanedai.net/sugiyama_65.php |title=杉山神社 (三浦郡葉山町) |date=20140808213836}}akanedai.net. Accessed January 12, 2020</ref> However, its relationship to the Sugiyama Shrines of Musashi Province remains unclear.
Another tradition, preserved at the {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Chigasaki Chūō, Tsuzuki Ward, Yokohama)|Q17994298}}, states that in the third year of the {{jalink|Hakuhō|Q11580612}} era under {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}}, three deities—{{jalink|Takami-Musubi|高御産日命}}, {{jalink|Ame-no-Hiwashi|Q10939697}}, and {{jalink|Abawakehiko-no-Mikoto|由布津主命}}—were enshrined on Sugiyama Hill in Musashi Province by Imibe no Katsumaro, a priest of {{jalink|Awa Shrine|Q705547}} of the {{jalink|Imbe clan|Q8189229}} from Kii Province. As the Imbe expanded their cultivation of hemp and grain, the shrine is said to have spread as well.
At that time, Tsuzuki District was strongly influenced by the Imbe clan, pioneer settlers who formed a small polity outside the control of the Musashi provincial government, constructing numerous {{jalink|kofun|Q1141225}} burial mounds and moated settlements. It also served as a distribution hub crossed by the ancient Tōkaidō route. However, although the *Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō* records that the shrine was founded by Imibe no Katsumaro, there are no surviving {{jalink|historical documentary sources|Q3771738}} to substantiate this claim, and it is considered weakly supported.<ref name="akanedai-sugiyama"/><ref name="yashiro-sugiyama" />
Separately from the above origin theories, there is also a tradition linking Sugiyama Shrine to {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}}. In Sugiyama of {{jalink|Doi District, Sagami Province|Q11423859}}, seven small shrines dedicated to {{jalink|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|Q11059454}} ({{jalink|Benzaiten}}) were established to protect the {{jalink|demon gate|Q11673539}} of {{jalink|Kamakura|Q10939227}}; these were collectively known as the “Seven Sugiyama Shrines.” Some of these overlap with candidate Engishiki Sugiyama Shrines.<ref>[http://hamarepo.com/story.php?story_id=1602? Yokohama City’s Scattered Sugiyama Shrines—A Complete Tour! vol.2] (Hamarepo.com, January 19, 2013)</ref>
Additionally, {{jalink|Ejima Sugiyama Shrine|Q97280595}} in {{jalink|Chitose, Sumida Ward|Q11405622}} of {{jalink|Tokyo|Q1490}} was originally a {{jalink|Benten shrine|弁天社}} (established by transferring a deity from {{jalink|Enoshima Shrine}}). The name “Sugiyama” derives from the enshrinement of {{jalink|Sugiyama Waichi|Q7635129}}.
=== Major Sugiyama Shrines in the Kantō Region === ; Yokohama City, Kanagawa Prefecture * {{jalink|Tsurumi Shrine (Yokohama)|Q11676163}} ({{jalink|Tsurumi Ward|Q1202820}}, {{jalink|Tsurumi-chūō|Q30924832}}) – former name * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Kishiya|Q11476016}}, Tsurumi Ward) * {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Katakura, Kanagawa Ward, Yokohama)|Q24863397}} * Sugiyama Ōkami ({{jalink|Rokkakubashi|Q11392542}}, Kanagawa Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Sugata-chō|Q96482916}}, Kanagawa Ward) * {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Nishi Ward, Yokohama)|Q11521946}} – also known as Tobe Sugiyama Shrine * {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Minami-Ōta, Yokohama)|Q134987090}} – also known as Yokohama Suitengū * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Miyamoto-chō|Q11453265}}, Minami Ward) * {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Kawashima-chō, Hodogaya Ward)|Q11521944}} – also known as Kawashima Sugiyama Shrine * {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Hoshikawa, Hodogaya Ward)|Q17994302}} – also known as Hoshikawa Sugiyama Shrine * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Wada|Q17216949}}, Hodogaya Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Hotoke-mukai-chō|Q11377506}}, Hodogaya Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Kamihoshikawa|Q11359094}}, Hodogaya Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Nishikubo-chō|Q85883633}}, Hodogaya Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Sakamoto-chō|Q97188018}}, Hodogaya Ward) * '''{{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Shin-Yoshida, Kōhoku Ward)|Q17994294}}''' – one of the leading candidate shrines * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Kishine-chō|Q11475945}}, Kōhoku Ward) * {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Niihama-chō, Kōhoku Ward)|}} – general guardian shrine of Niihama * Sugiyama Shrine (Tarumachi 4-chōme, Kōhoku Ward) * '''{{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Nishihassaku, Midori Ward)|Q11521945}}''' – one of the leading candidate shrines; participates in the {{jalink|Kurayami Festival|Q11265601}} of {{jalink|Ōkunitama Shrine|Q611678}} as the sixth-ranked shrine of Musashi Province * Sugiyama Shrine (Nakayama-chō, Midori Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Aoto-chō|Q28686651}}, Midori Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Kamoi|Q11675526}}, Midori Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Miho-chō|Q86744085}}, Midori Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine (Terayama-chō, Midori Ward) * {{jalink|Chigusadai Sugiyama Shrine|Q22119381}} ({{jalink|Aoba Ward|Q1203361}}) * {{jalink|Ichigao Sugiyama Shrine|Q22119100}} (Ichigao-chō, Aoba Ward) * {{jalink|Mitakedai Sugiyama Shrine}} (Mitakedai, Aoba Ward) * Kamionda Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Akane-dai|Q11256888}}, Aoba Ward) * Tetsu Shrine ({{jalink|Tetsu-chō|Q11649584}}, Aoba Ward) – formerly Aoki Myōjin and Sugiyama Myōjin combined * '''{{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Chigasaki, Tsuzuki Ward)|Q17994298}}''' – leading candidate shrine * '''{{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Nakagawa, Tsuzuki Ward)|Q17994300}}''' – leading candidate shrine; also known as Ōdana Sugiyama Shrine * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Katsuta-chō|Q97187870}}, Tsuzuki Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Ōkuma-chō|Q97188121}}, Tsuzuki Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Saedo-chō|Q13641379}}, Tsuzuki Ward) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Ikebe-chō|Q11552287}}, Tsuzuki Ward)
; Kanagawa Prefecture (outside Yokohama) * Sugiyama Ōkami ({{jalink|Ogura|Q11458882}}, {{jalink|Saiwai Ward|Q1153579}}, Kawasaki) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Suenaga|Q11519801}}, {{jalink|Takatsu Ward|Q1076736}}, Kawasaki) – shrine buildings destroyed by fire in {{jalink|1964|Q2652}} and rebuilt in {{jalink|1972|Q2476}} * Sugiyama Shrine (Nishi-Ikuta, {{jalink|Tama Ward|Q1076769}}, Kawasaki) * Sugiyama Shrine (Kamiyamaguchi, {{jalink|Hayama|Q1342807}}, Miura District)
; Tokyo Metropolis * {{jalink|Ejima Sugiyama Shrine|Q97280595}} ({{jalink|Chitose|Q11405622}}, {{jalink|Sumida Ward|Q235135}}) – enshrines Sugiyama Kengyō ({{jalink|Sugiyama Waichi|Q7635129}}) * Sugiyama Shrine (Hirao, Inagi) * {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Miwa-chō, Machida)|}} * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Naruse|Q11495526}}, Machida) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Kanamori|Q11647251}}, Machida) – two shrines in the same district * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Tsukushino|Q11272186}}, Machida) * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Nogaya|Q11611864}}, Machida) – Tsukushino branch office
=== Sugiyama Shrines outside the Kantō Region === * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Hyakuzawa|Q11580777}}, {{jalink|Hirosaki|Q194087}}, {{jalink|Aomori Prefecture|Q71699}}) * {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Sakurai City)|}} (Iimorizuka, {{jalink|Sakurai|Q127026}}, {{jalink|Nara Prefecture|Q131287}}) – enshrines {{jalink|Ōyamatsumi|大山祇神}}, with {{jalink|Ame-no-Minakanushi}} and {{jalink|Kamimusubi}} also enshrined * Sugiyama Shrine ({{jalink|Nishijin|Q11628294}}, {{jalink|Sawara Ward|Q1071779}}, {{jalink|Fukuoka City|Q26600}}, {{jalink|Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258}}) – an {{jalink|Inari shrine|Q514480}} enshrining {{jalink|Ukanomitama}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:杉山神社]] <!--generated by generate_shikinaisha_pages_v25_with_redirects.py--> {{Expand Japanese|杉山神社}}
{{Infobox religious building | name = Sukiyamano Shrine | native_name = 杉山神社 | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Sukiyamano Shrine | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }}
{{nihongo|'''Sukiyamano Shrine'''|杉山神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Musashi Province]].
== instance of (P31) ==
* {{ill|Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigesha|lt=Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigesha|WD=Q135038714|1=Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha}}<ref>Kokugakuin University Shrine database (Q135159299) https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=181691</ref> * {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|WD=Q134917286|1=Shikinaisha}} ** Series ordinal: 1140 * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Shikinai Shōsha|lt=Shikinai Shōsha|en|Shikinai Shōsha|ja|式内小社|simple|Shikinai Shōsha|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} * {{ill|Kokuhei-sha|lt=Kokuhei-sha|WD=Q135160342|1=Kokuhei-sha}}<ref>Kokugakuin University Shrine database (Q135159299) https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=181691</ref> * {{ill|Engishiki seat|lt=Engishiki seat|WD=Q135018062|1=Engishiki seat}}<ref>Kokugakuin University Shrine database (Q135159299) https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=181691</ref> ** Series ordinal: 1150 * {{ill|Unranked|lt=Unranked|ja|无位|WD=Q11504610|1=Unranked}}<ref>Kokugakuin University Shrine database (Q135159299) https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=181691</ref> * {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|ko|종5위|zh|從五位|WD=Q11071125|1=Junior Fifth Rank}}<ref>Kokugakuin University Shrine database (Q135159299) https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=181691</ref>
== part of (P361) ==

[[JA:杉山神社]]

{{translated page|ja|杉山神社|version=106747028|comment=Imported full ja history}}== Japanese content == '''Sugiyama Shrine''' (杉山神社, *Sugiyama-jinja*) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} that primarily enshrines {{jalink|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|Q10881556}} (a son of {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993}}) and {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} as its {{jalink|principal deities|主祭神}}.<ref name="akanedai-sugiyama"> {{Wayback |url=http://www.akanedai.net/sugiyama.php |title=杉山神社について |date=20140801182804}}akanedai.net. Accessed January 12, 2020</ref><ref>[http://rekisisuki.exblog.jp/15473809/ 杉山神社 大棚社 港北ニュータウン] (History and Pleasant Associations)</ref> It is considered one of the shrines listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Engishiki shrine register}} of the former {{jalink|Musashi Province|Q907398}}; however, there are several dozen shrines regarded as its {{jalink|candidate shrines|Q135022904}} (ronsha), mainly concentrated in present-day {{jalink|Yokohama|Q38283}} and elsewhere in {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}, as well as {{jalink|Kawasaki|Q164234}}, {{jalink|Machida|Q210628}}, and {{jalink|Inagi|Q317847}} in {{jalink|Tokyo Metropolis|Q1490}}.<ref name="akanedai-sugiyamalist"> {{Wayback |url=http://www.akanedai.net/sugiyama_list.php |title=杉山神社リスト |date=20170706050732}}akanedai.net. Accessed January 12, 2020</ref> The shrine name is sometimes written as '''椙山神社''' as well.
=== Overview === Sugiyama Shrine is said to have expanded primarily along the {{jalink|Tsurumi River|Q7850292}} {{jalink|river system|Q285451}} as a center of popular worship for people living in the surrounding region, but it remains a shrine with many unresolved mysteries. In addition to the Tsurumi River, shrines are found along the {{jalink|Katabira River|Q11481286}}, the {{jalink|Ōoka River (Kanagawa)|Q8081676}}, and on the right bank of the {{jalink|Tama River|Q1063006}} (in Kawasaki and Inagi), but none exist beyond the Tama River.
According to the *{{jalink|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|Q11503943}}*, compiled in the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, there were once 72 Sugiyama Shrines in total. Due to later {{jalink|shrine mergers|Q11412524}} and name changes, however, the number of shrines currently registered as {{jalink|religious corporations|Q7311343}} bearing the name “Sugiyama” (including the variant “Sugiyama/椙山”) stands at 44.<ref name="akanedai-sugiyama"/><ref name="akanedai-sugiyamalist"/><ref name="hodogayakyoudoshi-sugiyama">[http://www.shinmeisya.or.jp/rekisi/sugiyama_01.html 杉山神社考] (Shinmei Shrine: Hodogaya Local History)</ref>
In the *{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}*, compiled in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japanese era)|Q1075635}} 11 ({{jalink|869|Q23917}}) of the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, the shrine appears as “'''Fusuyama Shrine''' (枌山神社),” indicating its antiquity. The same text records that the shrine received {{jalink|imperial offerings|官幣}} in {{jalink|Jōwa (Japanese era)|Q1075605}} 5 ({{jalink|838|Q23838}}), second month, and was granted the rank of {{jalink|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|従五位下}} in Jōwa 15 ({{jalink|848|Q30380}}), fifth month.<ref name="hodogayakyoudoshi-sugiyama"/> Furthermore, the *{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}*, compiled in {{jalink|Engi (era)|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), describes it as “'''the sole Engishiki-listed shrine in Tsuzuki District of Musashi Province'''.” However, the location of the original main shrine has never been definitively identified, and many candidate shrines still exist. Among them, four shrines located in Yokohama—specifically in {{jalink|Midori Ward, Yokohama|Q1203373}} ({{jalink|Nishihassaku|Q86744095}}), {{jalink|Tsuzuki Ward|Q1153920}} (Nakagawa and {{jalink|Chigasaki Chūō|Q11617146}}), and {{jalink|Kōhoku Ward|Q1358965}} (Shin-Yoshida)—are currently regarded as the most plausible candidates.<ref name="yashiro-sugiyama">[http://skyimpulse.s26.xrea.com/sugiyama.html 杉山神社巡り] (Reflections on the Shrines of the Gods)</ref> {{See also|Tsurumi Shrine (Yokohama)#Sugiyama Shrine and Tsurumi Shrine}}
As for the origin of the name, several theories exist: that the shrine was enshrined on a cedar-covered mountain; that it derives from Isotakeru-no-Mikoto, a deity of trees, and cedar groves; or that it comes from cedar trees used as {{jalink|shipbuilding|船舶}} material.<ref name="tsugarukodai-sugiyama"> {{Wayback |url=http://www18.ocn.ne.jp/~tugaru/sikinaisyasugiyama.html |title=武蔵国都筑郡唯一の式内社杉山神社を推理 |date=20140810041733}} Accessed January 12, 2020 (Tsugaru Ancient Kingdom)</ref>
Although many aspects of the shrine’s origins remain unclear, one theory holds that descendants of the {{jalink|Izumo Province|Q582846}} people (Isotakeru-no-Mikoto) led a maritime group from {{jalink|Kii Province|Q864053}} {{jalink|Kumano|Q6443450}}, arrived at the {{jalink|Izu Peninsula|Q822822}} and {{jalink|Miura Peninsula|Q536757}}, and, via the latter, settled along the Tsurumi River system, where their leader founded Sugiyama Shrine.<ref name="tsugarukodai-sugiyama"/>
In {{jalink|Hayama, Miura District|Q1342807}} of the former {{jalink|Sagami Province|Q1053732}}, there exists the only Sugiyama Shrine in that province, along with a so-called “original shrine” (*motomiya*) enshrined in the surrounding mountains.<ref name="tsugarukodai-sugiyama"/><ref>{{Wayback |url=http://www.akanedai.net/sugiyama_65.php |title=杉山神社 (三浦郡葉山町) |date=20140808213836}}akanedai.net. Accessed January 12, 2020</ref> However, its relationship to the Sugiyama Shrines of Musashi Province remains unclear.
Another tradition, preserved at the {{jalink|Sugiyama Shrine (Chigasaki Chūō, Tsuzuki Ward, Yokohama)|Q17994298}}, states that in the third year of the {{jalink|Hakuhō|Q11580612}} era under {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}}, three deities—{{jalink|Takami-Musubi|高御産日命}}, {{jalink|Ame-no-Hiwashi|Q10939697}}, and {{jalink|Abawakehiko-no-Mikoto|由布津主命}}—were enshrined on Sugiyama Hill in Musashi Province by Imibe no Katsumaro, a priest of {{jalink|Awa Shrine|Q705547}} of the {{jalink|Imbe clan|Q8189229}} from Kii Province. As the Imbe expanded their cultivation of hemp and grain, the shrine is said to have spread as well.
At that time, Tsuzuki District was strongly influenced by the Imbe clan, pioneer settlers who formed a small polity outside the control of the Musashi provincial government, constructing numerous {{jalink|kofun|Q1141225}} burial mounds and moated settlements. It also served as a distribution hub crossed by the ancient Tōkaidō route. However, although the *Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō* records that the shrine was founded by Imibe no Katsumaro, there are no surviving {{jalink|historical documentary sources|Q3771738}} to substantiate this claim, and it is considered weakly supported.<ref name="akanedai-sugiyama"/><ref name="yashiro-sugiyama" />
Separately from the above origin theories, there is also a tradition linking Sugiyama Shrine to {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}}. In Sugiyama of {{jalink|Doi District, Sagami Province|Q11423859}}, seven small shrines dedicated to {{jalink|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|Q11059454}} ({{jalink|Benzaiten}}) were established to protect the {{jalink|demon gate|Q11673539}} of {{jalink|Kamakura|Q10939227}}; these were collectively known as the “Seven Sugiyama Shrines.” Some of these overlap with candidate Engishiki Sugiyama Shrines.<ref>[http://hamarepo.com/story.php?story_id=1602? Yokohama City’s Scattered Sugiyama Shrines—A Complete Tour! vol.2] (Hamarepo.com, January 19, 2013)</ref>
Additionally, {{jalink|Ejima Sugiyama Shrine|Q97280595}} in {{jalink|Chitose, Sumida Ward|Q11405622}} of {{jalink|Tokyo|Q1490}} was originally a {{jalink|Benten shrine|弁天社}} (established by transferring a deity from {{jalink|Enoshima Shrine}}). The name “Sugiyama” derives from the enshrinement of {{jalink|Sugiyama Waichi|Q7635129}}.
